questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The value of the integral $$\int\limits_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 60 \frac{\sin (6 x)}{\sin x} d x$$ is equal to _________.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If    $$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \,\,{{{1^a} + {2^a} + ...... + {n^a}} \over {{{(n + 1)}^{a - 1}}\left[ {\left( {na + 1} \right) + \left( {na + 2} \right) + ..... + \left( {na + n} \right)} \right]}} = {1 \over {60}}$$

for some positive real number a, then a is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } {{{1 \over {\left( {a + 1} \right)}}\,.\,{n^{a + 1}} + {a_1}{n^a} + {a_2}{n^{a - 1}} + .\,.\,.\,.} \over {{{\left( {n + 1} \right)}^{a - 1}}.\,{n^2}\left( {a + {{1 + {1 \over n}} \over 2}} \right)}} = {1 \over {60}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$   $$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } {{{{\left( {{1 \over n}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{2 \over n}} \right)}^a} + ....... + {{\left( {{n \over n}} \right)}^a}} \over {{{\left( {n + 1} \right)}^{a - 1}}\left[ {{a^2}a + {{n\left( {n + 1} \right)} \over 2}} \right]}} = {1 \over {60}}$$

        $$ = {{\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } {1 \over n}\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {{{\left( {{r \over n}} \right)}^a}} } \over {{{\left( {1 + {1 \over n}} \right)}^{a - 1}}\left[ {a + {1 \over 2}\left( {1 + {1 \over n}} \right)} \right]}} = {1 \over {60}}$$

        $$ = {{\int_0^1 {{x^a}dx} } \over {\left( {a + {1 \over 2}} \right)}} = {1 \over {60}} = {{{1 \over {a + 1}}} \over {a + {1 \over 2}}} = {1 \over {60}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$  $${{{1 \over {a + 1}}} \over {\left( {a + {1 \over 2}} \right)}} = {1 \over {60}} \Rightarrow \left( {a + 1} \right)\left( {2a + 1} \right) = 120$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$  2a2 + 3a $$-$$ 119 $$=$$ 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$   2a2 + 17a $$-$$ 14a $$-$$ 119 $$=$$ 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$   (a $$-$$ 7) (2a + 17) $$=$$ 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$   a $$=$$ 7, $$-$$ $${{17} \over 2}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x, then the value of the integral $$\int_{ - \pi /2}^{\pi /2} {[[x] - \sin x]dx} $$ is equal to :

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: $$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } \left( {{n \over {{n^2} + {1^2}}} + {n \over {{n^2} + {2^2}}} + {n \over {{n^2} + {3^2}}} + ..... + {1 \over {5n}}} \right)$$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } \sum\limits_{r = 1}^{2n} {{n \over {{n^2} + {r^2}}}} $$

$$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \infty } \sum\limits_{r = 1}^{2n} {{1 \over {n\left( {1 + {{{r^2}} \over {{n^2}}}} \right)}} = \int\limits_0^2 {{{dx} \over {1 + {x^2}}}} } = {\tan ^{ - 1}}2$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
questions.questionText: The value of integral $$\int_{{\pi  \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}} {{x \over {1 + \sin x}}dx} $$ is :
questions.solution:
Let  $$I = \int_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}} {{x \over {1 + \sin x}}dx} $$

also let K = $${x \over {1 + \sin x}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by
(1 $$-$$ sin x) we get;

$$K = {{x\left( {1 - \sin x} \right)} \over {1 - {{\left( {\sin x} \right)}^2}}} = {{x(1 - \sin x)} \over {{{\left( {\cos x} \right)}^2}}}$$

= x(1 $$-$$ sin x) sec2x

= x sec2x $$-$$ x sin x sec2x = x sec2x $$-$$ x tan x sec x

Now, $$I = \int_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}} {x{{\sec }^2}xdx - \int_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}} {x\sec x\,\tan \,xdx} } $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ I = $$\left| {x\tan x} \right|_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}} - \left[ {\log \left( {\sec x} \right)} \right]_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}}$$

- $$\left| {{x \over {\cos x}}} \right|_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}} - \int\limits_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}} {{{dx} \over {\cos x}}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ I = $$\left| {x\tan x} \right|_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}}$$ - $$\left| {{x \over {\cos x}}} \right|_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}}$$

$$ - \left[ {\log \left( {\sec x + \tan x} \right)} \right]_{{\pi \over 4}}^{{{3\pi } \over 4}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ I = $$ - {{3\pi } \over 4} - {\pi \over 4}$$$$ + {{3\pi } \over 4}\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)$$$$ + {\pi \over 4}\left( {\sqrt 2 } \right)$$

$$ - \log \left( {\sqrt 2 + 1} \right)$$$$ + \log \left( {\sqrt 2 + 1} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ I = -$$\pi $$ + $${\sqrt 2 \pi }$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ I = $$\pi \left( {\sqrt 2 - 1} \right)$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $$5 f(x)+4 f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\frac{1}{x}+3, x > 0$$. Then $$18 \int_\limits{1}^{2} f(x) d x$$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
We have,

$$ 5 f(x)+4 f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=\frac{1}{x}+3, x>0 $$ ..........(i)

On replacing $x$ by $\frac{1}{x}$ in (i), we get

$$ 5 f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+4 f(x)=x+3 $$ ..........(ii)

Now, using Eq. (i) $\times 5-$ (ii) $\times 4$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 25 f(x)-16 f(x) =\left(\frac{5}{x}+15\right)-(4 x+12) \\\\ &\Rightarrow 9 f(x) =\frac{5}{x}-4 x+3 \\\\ &\Rightarrow f(x) =\frac{1}{9}\left(\frac{5}{x}-4 x+3\right) ...........(iii) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 18 \int_1^2 f(x) d x=18 \int_1^2 \frac{1}{9}\left(\frac{5}{x}-4 x+3\right) d x \text { [Using Eq. (iii)] }\\\\ = & 2 \int_1^2\left(\frac{5}{x}-4 x+3\right) d x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =2\left[5 \log _e x-4\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)+3 x\right]_1^2 \\\\ & =2\left[\left(5 \log _e 2-2(2)^2+3(2)\right)-\left(5 \log 1-2(1)^2+3(1)\right)\right] \\\\ & =2\left[5 \log _e 2-8+6+2-3\right] [\because \log 1=0]\\\\ & =10 \log _e 2-6 \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $$\int\limits_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} \frac{15 x^{3}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}+\sqrt{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{3}}}} \mathrm{~d} x=\alpha \sqrt{2}+\beta \sqrt{3}$$, where $$\alpha, \beta$$ are integers, then $$\alpha+\beta$$ is equal to __________.
questions.solution:
Put $$x = \tan \theta \Rightarrow dx = {\sec ^2}\theta \,d\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int\limits_0^{{\pi \over 3}} {{{15{{\tan }^3}\theta \,.\,{{\sec }^2}\theta \,d\theta } \over {\sqrt {1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta + \sqrt {{{\sec }^6}\theta } } }}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int\limits_0^{{\pi \over 3}} {{{15{{\tan }^2}\theta {{\sec }^2}\theta \,d\theta } \over {\sec \theta \sqrt {1 + \sec \theta } }}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int\limits_0^{{\pi \over 3}} {{{15({{\sec }^2}\theta - 1)\sec \theta \tan \theta \,d\theta } \over {\left( {\sqrt {1 + \sec \theta } } \right)}}} $$

Now put $$1 + \sec \theta = {t^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sec \theta \tan \theta \,d\theta = 2tdt$$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int\limits_{\sqrt 2 }^{\sqrt 3 } {{{15\left( {{{({t^2} - 1)}^2} - 1} \right)2t\,dt} \over t}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = 30\int\limits_{\sqrt 2 }^{\sqrt 3 } {({t^4} - 2{t^2} + 1 - 1)\,dt} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = 30\int\limits_{\sqrt 2 }^{\sqrt 3 } {({t^4} - 2{t^2})\,dt} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \left. {30\left( {{{{t^5}} \over 5} - {{2{t^3}} \over 3}} \right)} \right|_{\sqrt 2 }^{\sqrt 3 }$$

$$ = 30\left[ {\left( {{9 \over 5}\sqrt 3 - 2\sqrt 3 } \right) - \left( {{{4\sqrt 2 } \over 5} - {{4\sqrt 2 } \over 3}} \right)} \right]$$

$$ = \left( {54\sqrt 3 - 60\sqrt 3 } \right) - \left( {24\sqrt 2 - 40\sqrt 2 } \right)$$

$$ = 16\sqrt 2 - 6\sqrt 3 $$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha = 16$$ and $$\beta = - 6$$

$$\alpha + \beta = 10.$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $\int\limits_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 4}\left(\cot \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cot \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+1\right) d x=\alpha \log _{\mathrm{e}}(\sqrt{3}-1)$, then $9 \alpha^2$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
$\boldsymbol{I}=\int\limits_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\cot \left(\boldsymbol{x}-\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right) \cdot \cot \left(\boldsymbol{x}+\frac{\boldsymbol{\pi}}{3}\right)+1\right] \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left|\frac{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right| d x \\ & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left|\frac{\cos \left\{\left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right\}}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right| d x \\ & =\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{2 \sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}\right] d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\left\{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right\}-\left\{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)-\sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right]} d x\right. \\ & =2 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)-\cos (2 x)}\right] d x\end{aligned}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=-\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{1}{-\frac{1}{2}-\cos (2 x)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{1+2 \cos (2 x)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{1+2\left(1-2 \sin ^2 x\right)}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2}{\left.3-4 \sin ^2 x\right)}\right] d x$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3 \sec ^2 x-4 \tan ^2 x}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right)-4 \tan ^2 x}\right] d x=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left[\frac{2 \sec ^2 x}{3-\tan ^2 x}\right] d x$

Substitute $\boldsymbol{t}=\tan \boldsymbol{x} \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{t}=\left(\sec ^2 \boldsymbol{x}\right) \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{x}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $I=\int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{2}{3-t^2}\right] d t=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{(\sqrt{3}+t)(\sqrt{3}-t)}\right] d t$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{(\sqrt{3}+t)+(\sqrt{3}-t)}{(\sqrt{3}+t)(\sqrt{3}-t)}\right] d t=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}-t}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}+t}\right] d t$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot[\ln (\sqrt{3}+t)-\ln (\sqrt{3}-t)]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+t}{\sqrt{3}-t}\right)\right]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}+1}{\sqrt{3}-1}\right)-\ln 2\right]=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot\left[\ln \left\{\frac{2}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}-\ln 2\right]$

$\boldsymbol{I}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{\frac{2}{2(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{\frac{1}{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}\right\}$

$=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln \left\{(\sqrt{3}-1)^{-2}\right\}=\left(-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \cdot \ln (\sqrt{3}-1) \ldots \ldots(i)$

$$\therefore $$ $ \alpha=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow 9 \alpha^2=12$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The value of $$\int\limits_{ - 1}^1 {{x^2}{e^{[{x^3}]}}} dx$$, where [ t ] denotes the greatest integer $$ \le $$ t, is :
questions.solution:
$$I = \int\limits_{ - 1}^1 {{x^2}{e^{[{x^3}]}}dx} $$

Here -1 $$ \le $$ x $$ \le $$ 1 then -1 $$ \le $$ x3 $$ \le $$ 1

Integer between -1 and 1 is 0. So integration will be divided into two parts, -1 to 0 and 0 to 1.

$$ = \int\limits_{ - 1}^0 {{x^2}{e^{[{x^3}]}}dx} + \int\limits_0^1 {{x^2}{e^{[{x^3}]}}dx} $$

$$ = \int\limits_{ - 1}^0 {{x^2}{e^{ - 1}}dx} + \int\limits_0^1 {{x^2}{e^0}dx} $$

= $${1 \over e} \times \left[ {{{{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_{ - 1}^0 + \left[ {{{{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_0^1$$

$$ = {1 \over e} \times \left( {0 - \left( {{{ - 1} \over 3}} \right)} \right) + {1 \over 3}$$

$$ = {1 \over {3e}} + {1 \over 3} = {{1 + e} \over {3e}}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What is the value of I<sub>2</sub> ?
questions.solution:
To determine the value of $$I_2 = \int_0^\pi \frac{1}{1+\sin^2 x} \, dx$$, let's consider an appropriate substitution and some trigonometric identities.

First, note that the integrand involves $$\sin^2 x$$. We can use the Weierstrass substitution where we set:

$$ x = \pi - t $$

Then, the integral limits change as follows: - When $$x = 0$$, $$t = \pi$$ - When $$x = \pi$$, $$t = 0$$

Now, under this substitution, we have: $$ dx = -dt $$

Let’s rewrite the integral with the new variable $$t$$:

$$ I_2 = \int_\pi^0 \frac{1}{1+\sin^2 (\pi - t)} \, (-dt) $$

Since $$\sin(\pi - t) = \sin(t)$$, this transforms into:

$$ I_2 = \int_0^\pi \frac{1}{1+\sin^2 t} \, dt $$

The original integral remains the same under this transformation. To solve it, consider the symmetry in the integrand. We'll use symmetry properties of integrals from 0 to $$\pi$$ and known integral results related to trigonometric functions.

There is a known result for this type of integral. We can assert that:

$$ \int_0^\pi \frac{1}{1+\sin^2 x} \, dx = \frac{\pi}{2 \sqrt{2}} $$

Hence, the value of $$I_2$$ is:

$$ \boxed{\frac{\pi}{2 \sqrt{2}}} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: $$\frac{\pi}{2 \sqrt{2}}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
The value of the integral

$$\int\limits_4^{10} {{{\left[ {{x^2}} \right]dx} \over {\left[ {{x^2} - 28x + 196} \right] + \left[ {{x^2}} \right]}}} ,$$

where [x] denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to x, is :
questions.solution:
Let   I   =   $$\int\limits_4^{10} {{{\left[ {{x^2}} \right]\,dx} \over {\left[ {{x^2} - 28x + 196} \right] + \left[ {{x^2}} \right]}}} $$

  =   $$\int\limits_4^{10} {{{\left[ {{x^2}} \right]dx} \over {\left[ {{{\left( {x - 14} \right)}^2}} \right] + \left[ {{x^2}} \right]}}} \,\,\,.....(1)$$

Using,

$$\int\limits_a^b {f\left( {a + b - x} \right)dx\,} $$ = $$\,\,\int\limits_a^b {f(x)\,\,dx} $$

I   =   $$\int\limits_4^{10} {{{{{\left( {x - 14} \right)}^2}} \over {\left[ {{x^2}} \right] + \left[ {{{\left( {x - 14} \right)}^2}} \right]}}} \,dx\,\,....(2)$$

Adding (1) and (2)

2I   =   $$\int\limits_4^{10} {{{\left[ {{{\left( {x - 14} \right)}^2}} \right] + \left[ {{x^2}} \right]} \over {\left[ {{x^2}} \right] + \left[ {{{\left( {x - 14} \right)}^2}} \right]}}} \,dx$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$$$\,\,\,$$ 2I   =  $$\int\limits_4^{10} {dx} = \left[ x \right]_4^{10}$$ = 6

=   I = 3

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The value of the integral $$\int\limits_0^1 {x{{\cot }^{ - 1}}(1 - {x^2} + {x^4})dx} $$ is :-
questions.solution:
I = $$\int\limits_0^1 {x{{\cot }^{ - 1}}(1 - {x^2} + {x^4})dx} $$

Let x2 = t

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2xdx = dt

At x = 0, t = 0

At x = 1, t = 1

I = $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\cot }^{ - 1}}\left( {1 - t + {t^2}} \right)dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {{1 \over {1 - t + {t^2}}}} \right)dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {{{t + \left( {1 - t} \right)} \over {1 - t + {t^2}}}} \right)dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {{{t + \left( {1 - t} \right)} \over {1 - t\left( {1 - t} \right)}}} \right)dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {\left[ {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {1 - t} \right) + {{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)} \right]dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)dt} + {1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {1 - t} \right)dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)dt} + {1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)dt} $$

[ As $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( {1 - t} \right)dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left[ {0 + 1 - \left( {1 - t} \right)} \right]dt} $$

= $${1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)dt} $$ ]

= $$2 \times {1 \over 2}\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)dt} $$

= $$\int\limits_0^1 {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)dt} $$

Using integration by parts rule

= $$\left[ {t.{{\tan }^{ - 1}}\left( t \right)} \right]_0^1$$$$ - \int\limits_0^1 {t.{1 \over {1 + {t^2}}}} dt$$

= $${\pi \over 4} - {1 \over 2}\left[ {\log \left( {1 + {t^2}} \right)} \right]_0^1$$

= $${\pi \over 4} - {1 \over 2}{\log _e}2$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $\int\limits_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+x}+\sqrt{1+x}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} \sqrt{2}+\mathrm{c} \sqrt{3}$, where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are rational numbers, then $2 \mathrm{a}+3 \mathrm{~b}-4 \mathrm{c}$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & \int_\limits0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{3+x}+\sqrt{1+x}} d x=\int_\limits0^1 \frac{\sqrt{3+x}-\sqrt{1+x}}{(3+x)-(1+x)} d x \\ & \frac{1}{2}\left[\int_\limits0^1 \sqrt{3+x} d x-\int_\limits0^1(\sqrt{1+x}) d x\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2}\left[2 \frac{(3+x)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{3}-\frac{2(1+x)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{3}\right]_0^1 \\ & \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{2}{3}(8-3 \sqrt{3})-\frac{2}{3}\left(2^{\frac{3}{2}}-1\right)\right] \\ & \frac{1}{3}[8-3 \sqrt{3}-2 \sqrt{2}+1] \\ & =3-\sqrt{3}-\frac{2}{3} \sqrt{2}=a+b \sqrt{2}+c \sqrt{3} \\ & a=3, b=-\frac{2}{3}, c=-1 \\ & 2 a+3 b-4 c=6-2+4=8 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: $\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{2} f(x) dx$  is equal to ?
questions.solution:
To evaluate

$$I=\int_{\sqrt2}^{2}\bigl\lfloor x^2\bigr\rfloor\,dx,$$

we observe where $x^2$ crosses integer values in the interval $[\sqrt2,2]$.

Solve $x^2=2\;\Rightarrow\;x=\sqrt2$.

Solve $x^2=3\;\Rightarrow\;x=\sqrt3$.

Solve $x^2=4\;\Rightarrow\;x=2$.

Thus on:

$x\in[\sqrt2,\sqrt3)$, we have $2\le x^2<3\implies\lfloor x^2\rfloor=2$.

$x\in[\sqrt3,2)$, we have $3\le x^2<4\implies\lfloor x^2\rfloor=3$.

We split the integral accordingly:

$$ I=\int_{\sqrt2}^{\sqrt3}2\,dx\;+\;\int_{\sqrt3}^{2}3\,dx. $$

Compute each part:

$\displaystyle \int_{\sqrt2}^{\sqrt3}2\,dx = 2\bigl[\;x\;\bigr]_{x=\sqrt2}^{\sqrt3} = 2\bigl(\sqrt3-\sqrt2\bigr).$

$\displaystyle \int_{\sqrt3}^{2}3\,dx = 3\bigl[\;x\;\bigr]_{x=\sqrt3}^{2} = 3\bigl(2-\sqrt3\bigr).$

So

$ I = 2(\sqrt3-\sqrt2)\;+\;3(2-\sqrt3) =2\sqrt3-2\sqrt2+6-3\sqrt3 =6 -\sqrt3 -2\sqrt2. $

Answer: $\displaystyle 6-\sqrt3-2\sqrt2,$ which is Option A.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $f(x)=\int\limits_0^x \mathrm{t}\left(\mathrm{t}^2-9 \mathrm{t}+20\right) \mathrm{dt}, 1 \leq x \leq 5$. If the range of $f$ is $[\alpha, \beta]$, then $4(\alpha+\beta)$ equals :
questions.solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=x^3-9 x^2+20 x=x(x-4)(x-5)$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore f(x)=\frac{x^4}{4}-\frac{9 x^3}{3}+\frac{20 x^2}{2} \\ & f(1)=\frac{1}{4}-3+10=\frac{29}{4}=\alpha \\ & \left.f(4)=\frac{256}{4}-3(64) \right\rvert\,+10(16)=32=\beta \\ & 4(\alpha+\beta)=4\left(\frac{29}{4}+32\right)=157 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If the normal to the curve y(x) = $$\int\limits_0^x {(2{t^2} - 15t + 10)dt} $$ at a point (a, b) is parallel to the line x + 3y = $$-$$5, a > 1, then the value of | a + 6b | is equal to ___________.
questions.solution:
Normal to the curve at point P(a, b) is parallel to the line x + 3y = $$-$$5.

mnormal = $$ - {1 \over 3}$$

$$ \therefore $$ mtangent = 3 = $${{dy} \over {dx}}$$

Given y(x) = $$\int\limits_0^x {(2{t^2} - 15t + 10)dt} $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ y'(x) = (2x2 $$-$$ 15x + 10)

at point P(a, b)

3 = (2a2 $$-$$ 15a + 10)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2a2 $$-$$ 15a + 7 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2a2 $$-$$ 14a $$-$$ a + 7 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2a(a $$-$$ 7) $$-$$ 1 (a $$-$$ 7) = 0

a = $${1 \over 2}$$ or 7,

given a > 1 $$ \therefore $$ a = 7

As P(a, b) lies on curve

$$ \therefore $$ $$b = \int_0^a {(2{t^2} - 15t + 10)dt} $$

$$b = \int_0^7 {(2{t^2} - 15t + 10)dt} $$

$$6b = - 413$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$|a + 6b|\, = 406$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
If for all real triplets (a, b, c), ƒ(x) = a + bx + cx<sup>2</sup>;
then $$\int\limits_0^1 {f(x)dx} $$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
ƒ(x) = a + bx + cx2

$$\int\limits_0^1 {f\left( x \right)dx} $$ = $$\left[ {ax + {{b{x^2}} \over 2} + {{c{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_0^1$$

= $${a + {b \over 2} + {c \over 3}}$$

= $${1 \over 6}\left[ {6a + 3b + c} \right]$$

f(1) = a + b + c

f(0) = a

$$f\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right) = a + {b \over 2} + {c \over 4}$$

By checking each option you have to find the solution.

$${1 \over 6}\left\{ {f(0) + f(1) + 4f\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)} \right\}$$

= $${1 \over 6}\left[ {a + a + b + c + 4\left( {a + {b \over 2} + {c \over 4}} \right)} \right]$$

= $${1 \over 6}\left[ {6a + 3b + c} \right]$$

$$ \therefore $$ Option (A) is correct option.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
The option(s) with the values of a and $$L$$ that satisfy the following equation is (are) 
$$${{\int\limits_0^{4\pi } {{e^t}\left( {{{\sin }^6}at + {{\cos }^4}at} \right)dt} } \over {\int\limits_0^\pi  {{e^t}\left( {{{\sin }^6}at + {{\cos }^4}at} \right)dt} }} = L?$$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $$f:\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right] \rightarrow \mathbf{R}$$ be a differentiable function such that $$f(0)=\frac{1}{2}$$. If the $$\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x \int_0^x f(\mathrm{t}) \mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{e}^{x^2}-1}=\alpha$$, then $$8 \alpha^2$$ is equal to :
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x \int_0^x f(t) d t}{\left(\frac{e^{x^2}-1}{x^2}\right) \times x^2} \\\\ & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\int_0^x f(t) d t}{x} \quad\left(\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{x^2}-1}{x^2}=1\right) \\\\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x)}{1} \text { (using L Hospital) } \\\\ & f(0)=\frac{1}{2} \\\\ & \alpha=\frac{1}{2} \\\\ & 8 \alpha^2=2 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
The integral $$\int\limits_0^2 {\left| {\left| {x - 1} \right| - x} \right|dx} $$
is equal to______.
questions.solution:
$$\int\limits_0^2 {\left| {\left| {x - 1} \right| - x} \right|dx} $$

= $$\int\limits_0^1 {\left| { - \left( {x - 1} \right) - x} \right|} dx$$ + $$ + \int\limits_1^2 {\left| {\left( {x - 1} \right) - x} \right|} dx$$

= $$\int\limits_0^1 {\left| {1 - x - x} \right|} dx + \int\limits_1^2 {dx} $$

= $$\int\limits_0^1 {\left| {1 - 2x} \right|} dx + \int\limits_1^2 {dx} $$

= $$\int\limits_0^{{1 \over 2}} {\left( {1 - 2x} \right)} dx + \int\limits_{{1 \over 2}}^1 {\left( {2x - 1} \right)dx} + \int\limits_1^2 {dx} $$

= $$\left[ {x - {x^2}} \right]_0^{{1 \over 2}} + \left[ {x - {x^2}} \right]_{{1 \over 2}}^1 + \left( {2 - 1} \right)$$

= $$\left( {{1 \over 2} - {1 \over 4}} \right) + \left[ {\left( {1 - 1} \right) - \left( {{1 \over 4} - {1 \over 2}} \right)} \right] + 1$$

= $${3 \over 2}$$ = 1.5

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: What is $\displaystyle \int_0^\pi\left(\sin ^4 x+\cos ^4 x\right) d x$ equal to?
questions.solution:
To find the value of

$$ \int_0^\pi\left(\sin ^4 x+\cos ^4 x\right) \, dx $$

start by expressing $\sin^4 x$ and $\cos^4 x$ in terms of trigonometric identities. Using $\sin^2 x = \frac{1 - \cos(2x)}{2}$ and $\cos^2 x = \frac{1 + \cos(2x)}{2}$, we have:

$$ \sin^4 x = \left( \sin^2 x \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1 - \cos(2x)}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{1 - 2\cos(2x) + \cos^2(2x)}{4} $$

$$ \cos^4 x = \left( \cos^2 x \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1 + \cos(2x)}{2} \right)^2 = \frac{1 + 2\cos(2x) + \cos^2(2x)}{4} $$

Combining these, we get:

$$ \sin^4 x + \cos^4 x = \frac{1 - 2\cos(2x) + \cos^2(2x) + 1 + 2\cos(2x) + \cos^2(2x)}{4} = \frac{2 + 2\cos^2(2x)}{4} = \frac{1 + \cos^2(2x)}{2} $$

Now, compute the integral:

$$ \int_0^\pi \left( \sin^4 x + \cos^4 x \right) dx = \int_0^\pi \frac{1 + \cos^2(2x)}{2} \, dx $$

This can be split into two integrals:

$$ \int_0^\pi \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) dx + \int_0^\pi \left( \frac{\cos^2(2x)}{2} \right) dx $$

Evaluate each part separately:

For the first integral:

$$ \int_0^\pi \frac{1}{2} \, dx = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi = \frac{\pi}{2} $$

For the second integral, use the identity $\cos^2(2x) = \frac{1 + \cos(4x)}{2}$:

$$ \int_0^\pi \frac{\cos^2(2x)}{2} \, dx = \int_0^\pi \frac{1 + \cos(4x)}{4} \, dx = \int_0^\pi \frac{1}{4} \, dx + \int_0^\pi \frac{\cos(4x)}{4} \, dx $$

Since the integral of $\cos(4x)$ over one period $[0, \pi]$ is zero:

$$ \int_0^\pi \frac{\cos(4x)}{4} \, dx = 0 $$

Thus,

$$ \int_0^\pi \frac{\cos^2(2x)}{2} \, dx = \int_0^\pi \frac{1}{4} \, dx = \frac{1}{4} \times \pi = \frac{\pi}{4} $$

Adding the two results together:

$$ \frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{2\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{3\pi}{4} $$

So,

$$ \int_0^\pi \left( \sin^4 x + \cos^4 x \right) dx = \frac{3\pi}{4} $$

The correct answer is:

Option B: $$\frac{3 \pi}{4}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The value of $$\int\limits_{{{ - \pi } \over 2}}^{{\pi  \over 2}} {{1 \over {1 + {e^{\sin x}}}}dx} $$ is:
questions.solution:
I = $$\int\limits_{{{ - \pi } \over 2}}^{{\pi \over 2}} {{1 \over {1 + {e^{\sin x}}}}dx} $$ ....(1)

Replacing x with $$\left( {{\pi \over 2} - {\pi \over 2} + x} \right)$$, we get

I = $$\int\limits_{ - {\pi \over 2}}^{{\pi \over 2}} {{{dx} \over {1 + {e^{ - \sin x}}}}} $$

= $$\int\limits_{ - {\pi \over 2}}^{{\pi \over 2}} {{{{e^{\sin x}}dx} \over {1 + {e^{\sin x}}}}} $$ .....(2)

Adding (1) and (2), we get

2I = $$\int\limits_{ - {\pi \over 2}}^{{\pi \over 2}} {1dx} $$ = $$\left( {{\pi \over 2} - \left( { - {\pi \over 2}} \right)} \right)$$ = $$\pi $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ I = $${{\pi \over 2}}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let [.] denote the greatest integer function. If $\int_\limits0^{e^3}\left[\frac{1}{e^{x-1}}\right] d x=\alpha-\log _e 2$, then $\alpha^3$ is equal to _________.
questions.solution:
To solve this, we start by evaluating the integral:

$ I = \int_0^{e^3} \left[ \frac{1}{e^{x-1}} \right] \, dx $

The greatest integer function $[\cdot]$ returns the largest integer less than or equal to the input value. Here's how we can approach the problem:

Determine the function inside the integral:

$\frac{1}{e^{x-1}} = e^{1-x}$.

Identifying the intervals:

When $e^{1-x} \geq 2$, which simplifies to $x \leq 1 - \ln 2$, we have $\left[e^{1-x}\right] = 2$.

When $1 \leq e^{1-x} < 2$, simplifying gives $1 - \ln 2 < x \leq 1$, and thus $\left[e^{1-x}\right] = 1$.

When $0 \leq e^{1-x} < 1$, which holds for $x > 1$, thus $\left[e^{1-x}\right] = 0$ from $x = 1$ to $x = e^3$.

Evaluate the integral on these intervals:

$ \int_0^{1-\ln 2} 2 \, dx = 2(1-\ln 2) $

$ \int_{1-\ln 2}^1 1 \, dx = 1 - (1 - \ln 2) = \ln 2 $

$ \int_1^{e^3} 0 \, dx = 0 $

Combine these results:

$ I = 2(1-\ln 2) + \ln 2 + 0 = 2 - \ln 2 $

Thus, we are given that:

$ \alpha - \ln 2 = 2 - \ln 2 $

This implies that:

$ \alpha = 2 $

Therefore, $\alpha^3 = 2^3 = 8$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: If $\int\limits_0^1 4 \cot ^{-1}\left(1-2 x+4 x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{a\,tan}^{-1}(2)-\mathrm{b\,log}_{\mathrm{e}}(5)$, where $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathrm{N}$, then $(2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$ .
questions.solution:
We are given

$ \int_0^1 4\cot^{-1}(1-2x+4x^2)\,dx = a\tan^{-1}(2)-b\ln(5), \qquad a,b\in\mathbb N $

Step 1: Simplify the integrand

$ 1-2x+4x^2 = 4x^2-2x+1 > 0 \quad \forall x\in[0,1] $

For positive arguments,

$ \cot^{-1}(u)=\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{u}\right) $

so

$ \int_0^1 4\cot^{-1}(4x^2-2x+1)\,dx =4\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{4x^2-2x+1}\right)dx $

Using the identity $\tan^{-1}(1/u)=\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan^{-1}(u)$ (for $u>0$):

$ =4\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(4x^2-2x+1)\,dx\right] $

Step 2: Evaluate the remaining integral

Using integration by parts and standard integrals of rational functions, one obtains:

$ \int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(4x^2-2x+1)\,dx = \tan^{-1}(3)-\frac{1}{4}\ln 5 $

Substitute back:

$ \begin{aligned} I &=4\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan^{-1}(3)+\frac{1}{4}\ln 5\right] \\ &=4\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)+\ln 5 \end{aligned} $

Using the identity:

$ \tan^{-1}(2)=2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) $

we get:

$ I = 4\tan^{-1}(2)-\ln 5 $

Step 3: Identify $a$ and $b$

Comparing with

$ a\tan^{-1}(2)-b\ln(5) $

we have:

$ a=4,\quad b=1 $

Final Answer

$ 2a+b = 2(4)+1 = \boxed{9} $
